text: Hello, I'm sharing another robotic sigmoid with transrectal extraction of specimen and some anastomosis. It's a patient with favorable body habitus. The patient had had several attacks of diverticulitis. The most recent one was about five months prior. Believe it or not, there was an abscess. It did not require drainage, just antibiotics.
I was actually surprised how normal everything looked intra-abdominally, but It doesn't change what the patient symptomatically had.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, preparing for high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) to achieve optimal length for the anastomosis.
The surgeon skeletonizes the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and creates a window behind it before dividing the vessel using a vessel sealer.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection out to the sidewall, following their preferred flow of the operation.
The surgeon places a sponge underneath the dissected area to protect the ureter and proceeds with the lateral dissection, following the visible white line on the tissue.
Stay right on that interface. In this case, it was quite easy. There are no particular challenges or uniqueness with this case as the pathology at the time of surgery looked relatively normal.

The surgeon maintains traction while performing lateral dissection, aiming to enter the previously dissected medial plane. The procedure involves steps of medial to lateral dissection followed by transrectal extraction with sononastomosis.
The surgeon identifies the ureter during the dissection, noting the absence of stents in this case.

The surgeon enters the medially dissected plane, revealing the psoas tendon and ureter. The procedure continues with lateral mobilization towards the splenic flexure, although full mobilization is not shown.

The surgeon dissects off the left pelvic sidewall down to the upper rectum, enhancing mobility for the upcoming anastomosis. The distal transection margin is selected, followed by the search for the proximal margin.

The surgeon clears the distal upper rectal mesentery, revealing the selected area for the proximal margin. The dissection proceeds cephalad towards the transection site of the inferior mesenteric artery, moving up to the proximal margin.
The surgeon assesses perfusion using indocyanine green (ICG) after taking the mesentery to both planned margins. The distal and proximal transections are then performed.

When specimens are too large to extract as a whole piece, I will then chop it up into smaller pieces. I have never really found much success with the placement of a transrectal wound protector. I have seen many demonstrated videos of this, which are absolutely beautiful. I just haven't had the same success myself. Additionally, I'm working without residents and I don't have a separate person to go down below, so I'm doing all of this myself. And I just found this to be the easiest way for me to do it. I'm usually transecting specimens, taking the mesentery off when I've done a high ligation. and then transecting the specimen into three or four smaller pieces. The whole thing takes maybe ten minutes.

The surgeon begins a hand-sewn single-layer anastomosis using a 3-0 silk suture on the far left lateral side for traction and manipulation. The suture is placed in the posterior midline and progresses towards the left side.

I can individually place these sutures and pull to the tension that I like, which is the explicit reason why I really like these sewn anastomosis and not relying on surgical staplers. I have zero evidence to back up my statement. This part of the procedure certainly takes longer than stapling. I would say that this takes me between twenty and twenty-five minutes these days. What you're saving in time is not having to make a fan and steel incision and then a fan and steel closure. Especially if you are still doing extracorporeal anastomosis stapled, you'd be undocking the robot and then redocking or finishing laparoscopically. So it's kind of time neutral. I'm hopeful that I will get faster with my anastomosis though.

The surgeon begins with a three OB lock suture in the midline, stitching towards the near side using an over-and-over technique on the posterior row. As the suture reaches the anterior side, the surgeon transitions to an inverting Limbert suture, carefully working back towards the starting point to avoid the confluence of sutures near a corner.

The surgeon inverts the two lips of mucosa during the anastomosis, adding an additional suture throw for reinforcement. The sutures are tied together, completing the anastomosis, followed by a leak test to ensure integrity.

The surgeon demonstrates the completed anastomosis, showing no tension on the sutured area. The pelvis is flooded to test for leaks, and a rigid scope is inserted below to inspect the anastomosis. The scope reveals no bubbles, indicating a successful leak test, and the anastomosis is confirmed to be widely patent.
